seo.title: Cybersecurity AI Email Assistant AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for ai email assistant AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying an AI email assistant in a cybersecurity environment introduces severe risks including adversarial intelligence leakage, automated escalation of incidents, and compliance violations under NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001. This checklist ensures your AI email agent operates within strict security boundaries, maintains human oversight for critical actions, and protects sensitive threat intelligence from exposure through LLM APIs or generated content. Following these controls is essential to prevent adversary exploitation, avoid false-positive-driven outages, and maintain auditability across all automated email workflows.

sections.heading: Data Handling & Threat Intelligence Protection
sections.description: Ensure the AI email agent never exposes classified threat intelligence, IOCs, CVEs, or internal vulnerability data through LLM API calls, generated drafts, or attachment processing pipelines.

items.label: Sanitize inputs before LLM API transmission
items.description: Strip all IOCs (IP addresses, hashes, domains), CVE identifiers, internal asset names, and classified threat intelligence from email content before sending to any external LLM API endpoint. Implement a pre-processing filter layer that redacts sensitive fields using regex and NLP classifiers.

items.label: Enforce data residency for threat intelligence processing
items.description: Ensure that emails containing threat intelligence data are processed only by on-premises or private cloud LLM deployments. Document data flow diagrams showing that classified threat data never traverses public LLM API endpoints, in compliance with CISA directives on sensitive data handling.

items.label: Implement attachment sandboxing before AI analysis
items.description: Route all email attachments through an isolated sandbox environment (e.g., Cuckoo, Any.run integration) before the AI agent processes or summarizes their content. Prevent malicious attachments from injecting adversarial prompts into the AI pipeline.

items.label: Classify email sensitivity tiers before AI processing
items.description: Apply automated sensitivity classification (e.g., TLP:RED, TLP:AMBER, CONFIDENTIAL) to inbound emails before the AI agent processes them. Restrict AI-generated drafts and summaries based on classification level, blocking AI responses to TLP:RED emails entirely without human initiation.

items.label: Audit all data sent to LLM APIs
items.description: Log every payload transmitted to LLM APIs with timestamps, user context, and content hashes. Store logs in a SIEM-integrated, tamper-evident system to support SOC 2 Type II audit requirements and enable forensic review if intelligence leakage is suspected.

items.label: Implement PII detection and redaction pipeline
items.description: Deploy a dedicated PII detection layer (e.g., Microsoft Presidio or AWS Comprehend) that identifies and redacts names, email addresses, phone numbers, and employee IDs from email content before AI processing and before draft generation is finalized.

items.label: Restrict AI access to internal threat intelligence platforms
items.description: Use least-privilege API scopes when integrating the AI email agent with threat intel platforms (e.g., MISP, ThreatConnect). The agent should have read-only access limited to non-classified indicators, with no ability to export or embed raw threat feeds into outbound emails.

sections.heading: Human Approval Workflows & Automated Action Controls
sections.description: Establish mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoints for all AI-generated email actions, especially those triggering incident response workflows, security advisories, or communications to external parties.

items.label: Require human approval for all outbound security advisory emails
items.description: Configure the send approval workflow to block all AI-generated security advisories, vulnerability notifications, and incident communications from being sent without explicit approval from a designated security analyst or CISO. Implement a dual-approval requirement for advisories affecting critical infrastructure systems.

items.label: Block automated send for emails triggering incident response actions
items.description: Detect when AI-drafted emails contain language that initiates IR actions (e.g., 'isolate system', 'revoke credentials', 'block IP') and enforce a mandatory human review gate. Integrate with your SOAR platform to flag these drafts and route them to the on-call SOC analyst before dispatch.

items.label: Implement recipient validation before AI draft finalization
items.description: Validate all recipient email addresses against an approved internal directory and external partner allowlist before the AI agent finalizes a draft. Flag and quarantine any draft addressed to unrecognized external domains, particularly those resembling adversary-controlled infrastructure.

items.label: Define and enforce AI action permission boundaries
items.description: Document a formal policy specifying which email actions the AI agent can perform autonomously (e.g., draft creation, meeting scheduling) versus which require human approval (e.g., sending to external parties, attaching sensitive reports). Enforce these boundaries programmatically in the agent's action execution layer.

items.label: Implement time-bounded approval windows for urgent security emails
items.description: For time-sensitive security communications, configure approval workflows with defined SLA windows (e.g., 15-minute escalation for P1 incidents). If approval is not granted within the window, automatically escalate to the next approval tier rather than allowing the AI to send autonomously.

items.label: Log all human approval and rejection decisions with rationale
items.description: Capture every approval, rejection, and modification made to AI-generated email drafts, including the approver identity, timestamp, and any edits made. Store these records for a minimum of 12 months to satisfy SOC 2 Type II change management and ISO 27001 audit trail requirements.

items.label: Provide approvers with AI confidence scores and source citations
items.description: Display the AI agent's confidence score, the source data used to generate the draft, and any CVE or threat intel references alongside each draft awaiting approval. This enables approvers to make informed decisions rather than rubber-stamping AI output.

sections.heading: Content Accuracy, Verification & Compliance Enforcement
sections.description: Validate that AI-generated email content is factually accurate, appropriately toned, and compliant with NIST CSF, ISO 27001, and CISA directives before any email reaches its recipient.

items.label: Verify CVE references against NVD and MITRE databases before sending
items.description: Implement an automated post-generation check that queries the NIST National Vulnerability Database (NVD) and MITRE CVE database to validate every CVE identifier mentioned in AI-generated drafts. Reject or flag drafts containing unverified, deprecated, or incorrectly described CVEs.

items.label: Enforce tone appropriateness classifier for security communications
items.description: Run all AI-generated drafts through a tone and language classifier trained on cybersecurity communication standards. Flag drafts that use alarmist language, make unverified claims about breach severity, or use language that could cause panic-driven actions by recipients.

items.label: Cross-reference AI security recommendations against approved playbooks
items.description: Before finalizing any AI-generated email containing remediation steps or security recommendations, automatically compare the suggested actions against your organization's approved IR playbooks and NIST SP 800-61 guidelines. Block recommendations that contradict established procedures.

items.label: Implement confidentiality footer enforcement
items.description: Automatically append appropriate confidentiality notices (e.g., TLP classification banners, legal disclaimers) to all AI-generated outbound emails based on content classification. Ensure the AI agent cannot generate emails that omit required confidentiality markings mandated by your ISO 27001 information classification policy.

items.label: Validate that AI-generated emails do not contain hallucinated threat data
items.description: Deploy a factual grounding check that compares threat actor names, malware families, and attack technique references in AI drafts against authoritative sources (MITRE ATT&CK, VirusTotal). Flag any references not found in verified threat intelligence sources as potential hallucinations requiring human review.

items.label: Enforce character and link scanning on AI-generated email bodies
items.description: Scan all AI-generated email bodies for embedded URLs, encoded payloads, and suspicious Unicode characters that could indicate prompt injection attacks influencing the AI to insert malicious content. Integrate with your email security gateway (e.g., Proofpoint, Mimecast) for outbound scanning.

items.label: Conduct periodic red-team testing of AI email content generation
items.description: Schedule quarterly adversarial testing exercises where your red team attempts to manipulate the AI email agent into generating emails that leak threat intelligence, bypass approval workflows, or produce misleading security advisories. Document findings and remediate identified weaknesses.

sections.heading: Access Control, Monitoring & Incident Response Readiness
sections.description: Establish robust identity controls, continuous behavioral monitoring, and a documented incident response plan specifically for AI email agent failures, misuse, or compromise.

items.label: Enforce role-based access control for AI email agent capabilities
items.description: Define granular RBAC policies that restrict which users can configure the AI agent, approve drafts, access email processing logs, and modify intent detection rules. Align roles with your ISO 27001 access control policy and enforce MFA for all administrative access to the AI email platform.

items.label: Integrate AI email agent activity into SIEM for real-time monitoring
items.description: Stream all AI agent actions (draft creation, approval requests, sends, rejections, API calls) to your SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel). Create detection rules for anomalous patterns such as bulk draft generation, repeated approval bypasses, or unusual recipient domains.

items.label: Implement an AI agent kill switch with documented activation procedure
items.description: Deploy an emergency shutdown mechanism that allows the SOC to immediately disable the AI email agent's send and draft capabilities without disrupting the underlying email infrastructure. Document the activation procedure in your IR playbook and test it during tabletop exercises.

items.label: Define and test an incident response plan for AI email agent compromise
items.description: Create a dedicated IR playbook for scenarios where the AI email agent is compromised, manipulated via prompt injection, or begins generating malicious content. Include containment steps, forensic evidence collection procedures, and notification requirements under CISA reporting directives.

items.label: Monitor for prompt injection attempts in inbound emails
items.description: Deploy a prompt injection detection layer that scans inbound email content for patterns designed to manipulate the AI agent's behavior (e.g., 'Ignore previous instructions', hidden text in HTML). Alert the SOC when injection attempts are detected and quarantine the offending email.

items.label: Conduct SOC 2 Type II evidence collection for AI agent operations
items.description: Establish automated evidence collection processes that capture AI agent operational logs, approval workflow records, and configuration change history in formats suitable for SOC 2 Type II audits. Ensure evidence is retained for a minimum of 12 months and stored in an immutable log repository.

items.label: Perform quarterly access reviews for AI email agent permissions
items.description: Review and recertify all user and service account permissions associated with the AI email agent every quarter. Revoke access for departed employees, contractors, and unused service accounts immediately, and document reviews to satisfy ISO 27001 A.9 access control audit requirements.

items.label: Establish behavioral baseline and anomaly alerting for AI email patterns
items.description: Establish a behavioral baseline for normal AI email agent activity (average drafts per hour, typical recipient domains, common intent categories) and configure alerts for deviations exceeding defined thresholds. Use this baseline to detect potential compromise or misuse of the AI agent.

notes.note: The AI email agent must be mapped to NIST CSF 2.0 functions: GOVERN (establish AI usage policies), IDENTIFY (classify email data assets and AI risks), PROTECT (enforce access controls and data sanitization), DETECT (monitor for anomalous AI behavior), RESPOND (activate IR playbooks for AI failures), and RECOVER (restore normal email operations post-incident). Document this mapping in your organization's CSF profile and review it annually or after significant AI capability changes.

The AI email assistant must satisfy SOC 2 Type II Trust Services Criteria, particularly CC6 (Logical and Physical Access Controls), CC7 (System Operations and Monitoring), and CC9 (Risk Mitigation). Auditors will require evidence of continuous monitoring of AI agent actions, documented approval workflows, access control reviews, and incident response testing. Ensure all AI-generated email activity is logged in tamper-evident systems and that approval workflow records are retained for the full audit period.

Under ISO 27001:2022 Annex A controls, the AI email agent must comply with A.5.12 (Information Classification) by enforcing TLP and sensitivity labels on all AI-processed emails, A.8.20 (Networks Security) by ensuring threat intelligence data does not traverse unsecured API channels, and A.5.23 (Information Security for Use of Cloud Services) by documenting LLM API vendor risk assessments. Additionally, A.6.8 (Information Security Event Reporting) requires that AI agent anomalies and prompt injection attempts be reported through your established incident management process.

CISA Binding Operational Directives (BODs) and Emergency Directives require that federal and critical infrastructure operators protect threat intelligence from unauthorized disclosure. When the AI email agent processes emails containing CISA-shared threat intelligence (e.g., from AIS or ISAC feeds), this data must never be transmitted to external LLM APIs. Additionally, CISA's guidance on AI cybersecurity (per the 2023 CISA AI Roadmap) mandates human oversight for AI-driven security actions, directly requiring the human approval workflows specified in this checklist for any automated response actions initiated via email.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI email agent against NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 controls—automatically flagging compliance gaps, enforcing approval workflows, and generating audit-ready evidence before your next security review.
